coredata.dc:title: Bipolar magnetic semiconductor properties and spin-dependent Seebeck effects induced by nanoscale graphene domains doped into armchair boron nitride nanoribbons
coredata.dc:creator: Tan, Xingyi
Zhang, Lina
Liu, Lili
coredata.dc:description: Inspired by the synthesis of nanometer-scale graphene quantum dots via a doping process in boron nitride (BN) sheets (Adv. Mater. 2019, 1805778), we investigated the magnetic and thermal spin transport properties of armchair BN nanoribbons containing nanoscale graphene domains. It was found that both M-QB and M-QN structures were bipolar magnetic semiconductors (BMSs). Two spin caloritronics devices were designed on the basis of the 15-6B and 15-6N nanoribbons. Spin-dependent Seebeck effects, spin-dependent Seebeck diode effects and thermoelectric diode effects were observed in these devices. This work provides theoretical guidance for the design of new spin caloritronics devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Spin caloritronics
Bipolar magnetic semiconductors
BN nanoribbons
Nanoscale graphene domains
Spin-dependent Seebeck effect

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Crystal structure diagrams of M-QB: from left to right, 15-6B, 15-8B, and 15-10B. (b) Crystal structure diagrams of M-QN: from left to right, 15-6N, 15-8N, and 15-10N. (c) Schematic illustration of thermal spin caloritronics device based on armchair-type BN nanoribbons doped with nanoscale graphene domains. The spin-dependent currents are induced by ΔT, i.e., the difference between the temperatures of the left electrode (T L) and the right electrode (T R), given by ΔT = T L − T R. Brown, blue and gray balls represent the B, N and C atoms, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Band structures of M-QB at Q = 6 (M = 12–29).
Band structures of M-QN at Q = 6 (M = 12–29).
Band structures of M-QB and M-QN at M = 15 (Q = 6, 8, 10).
Spin electron density maps: (a) from left to right, 15-6B, 15-8B, and 15-10B; (b) from left to right, 15-6N, 15-8N, and 15-10N. The isosurface value is set to 0.01 e/Å3 for all cases.
Thermal spin current of the 15-6B device as a function of (a) T L and (d) ΔT; (b, c) change curves for I c and I s with T L; (e, f) change curves for I c and I s with ΔT.
Thermal spin current of the 15-6N device as a function of (a) T L and (d) ΔT; (b, c) change curves for I c and I s with T L; (e, f) change curves for I c and I s with ΔT.

Spin transmission spectra of the 15-6B (a) and 15-6N (b) devices, where the black line indicates spin-up and the red line indicates spin-down. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Bipolar magnetic semiconductor properties and spin-dependent Seebeck effects induced by nanoscale graphene domains doped into armchair boron nitride nanoribbons

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Chongqing Three Gorges University, Wanzhou 404100, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Chongqing Three Gorges University, Wanzhou 404100, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physical Science and Technology, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physical Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physical Science and Technology, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Inspired by the synthesis of nanometer-scale graphene quantum dots via a doping process in boron nitride (BN) sheets (Adv. Mater. 2019, 1805778), we investigated the magnetic and thermal spin transport properties of armchair BN nanoribbons containing nanoscale graphene domains. It was found that both M-QB and M-QN structures were bipolar magnetic semiconductors (BMSs). Two spin caloritronics devices were designed on the basis of the 15-6B and 15-6N nanoribbons. Spin-dependent Seebeck effects, spin-dependent Seebeck diode effects and thermoelectric diode effects were observed in these devices. This work provides theoretical guidance for the design of new spin caloritronics devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Spin caloritronics, a reseach field focusing on the interplayer of spin and heat currents, has been attracting increasing attention over the last two decades [1–8]. Potential applications of this filed include thermoelectric conversion and information processing technologies [9–18]. Historically, the concept of spin thermoelectronics was initially proposed by Johnson and Silsbee in the later 1980s [19,20]. However, this field has become again a topic of keen interest for researchers since the spin Seebeck effect was observed in the ferromagnetic metal alloy NiFe in 2008 by Uchida et al. [3]. Since then, many new spin caloritronics devices have been theoretical designed and many physical effects have been discovered, including spin-dependent Seebeck diode (SDSD) effects, the negative differential spin-Seebeck effect, the spin-dependent Seebeck effect (SDSE), and the thermal colossal magnetoresistance effect [21–25]. However, to date, successful experimental examples of spin caloritronics devices are rare, due to the lack of high quality ferromagnetic nanoribbons synthesized. Therefor, the more practicable spin caloritronics devices still need to be explored.

Over the past few years, graphene and monolayer hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN, which is known as white graphene) have been widely studied because they have the same atomically flat, layered structure with a honeycomb lattice but exhibit different properties [26–30]. Graphene has extremely high electron mobility, high thermal conductivity and long spin diffusion lengths (~2 µm), which makes it a suitable candidate material for electronic and spin-related thermoelectric device applications [26,31–33]. However, the lack of a band gap in pristine graphene greatly limits the applications of graphene in two-dimensional electronics. In contrast, h-BN is an insulator with a wide band gap of 6.0 eV [34] and plays an important role in many areas in basic science and technology, including gate dielectrics, contact resistance and atomic tunneling. The structural similarities and differing electronic structures of graphene and h-BN have stimulated great interest in the synthesis of two-dimensional hexagonal boron–carbon-nitrogen (2D h-BCN) atomic layers. Recently, some researches were carried out on the synthesis of 2D h-BCN [35–42]. For example, Ajayan et al. [36]. synthesized large-area atomic layers of h-BCN that consisted of randomly distributed domains composed of carbon phases in pure h-BN. Cheng et al. [35]. found that graphene and h-BN nanodomains can exist in h-BCN layers. Importantly, Fan et al. [38]. demonstrated that nanometer-scale graphene quantum dots can be inserted into h-BN sheets as complete units and also observed ferromagnetism in these structures. Similar to graphene nanoribbons and h-BN nanoribbons, h-BCN nanosheet could be tailored into nanoribbons. In this paper, the electronic structurs and thermal spin transport properties of armchair-type BN nanoribbons with nanoscale graphene domains (BNCs) are studied using density function theory in combination with the non-equilibrium Green’s function method. The results show that all the nanoribbons presented here have a robust bipolar magnetic semiconductors characteristic. Applying a temperature gradient, the SDSE and SDSD effects are achieved. This provides theoretical guidance for the preparation of low-energy spin electronic devices.

Fig. 1 shows crystal structure diagrams of the armchair-type BNCs. These structures are labeled as M-QB and M-QN, where M is the number of BN chains along the direction of the nanoribbons, Q is the number of BN chains in the direction of the vertical nanoribbons, and B and N are the atoms that are connected to the carbon atoms. For example, the first graph shown in Fig. 1(a) can be represented using this notation as 15-6B. Fig. 1(c) shows a schematic illustration of a thermal spin caloritronics device based on the armchair-type BNCs. The left electrode and the right electrode each contain one unit of M-QB or M-QN, while the central region contains three units of M-QB or M-QN. To study the effects of temperature on the spin transport, the temperature of the left electrode (T L) is constantly kept higher than the temperature of the right electrode (T R), and the temperature difference is ΔT = T L − T R.

The calculations were performed using the Atomistix Toolkit (ATK) package [43,44], which uses spin density functional theory in combination with the non-equilibrium Green’s function method. The geometrical optimization and electronic structure calculations are performed using the single zeta polarized (SZP) basis set and the exchange correlation potential is treated using the local-density approximation (LDA) method implemented in ATK [45,46]. The spatial positions of the atoms remain relaxed until the interaction force between the atoms is less than 0.05 eV/Å, the energy convergence standard when the structure converges is 2 × 10−4 eV, and the cutoff energy is 150 Ha. After structural optimization, two devices were designed based on 15-6B and 15-6N. The same core electrons and exchange correlation potential were selected to calculate the thermal spin-dependent currents. The k-point sampling numbers in the x, y and z directions were 1, 1 and 100, respectively, and the cut-off energy was 150 Ha.

In the LandauerBüttiker formalism, the spin-dependent currents of the devices can be obtained using the following equation [47]:
(1)
I
↑

↑

f L
E ,

d E

In the formula, e is the electron charge, and h is the Planck constant. TL
(
R
) is the temperature of the left (right) electrode, and fL
(

)(E,TL
(

)) is the average Fermi-Dirac distribution of the left (right) electrode, as defined below:
(2)
f

In the above formula,

are the chemical potential and the temperature of the left (right) electrode, respectively, and

is the Boltzmann constant. T↑(↓)(E) is the spin-dependent transport coefficient and is defined as:
(3)

G a
here, Gr(a) is a Green’s function that is retarded in the central region and ΓL(R) is the coupling matrix for the left (right) electrode.

The band structures of M-QB and M-QN are shown in Figs. 2, 3 and 4
. For M-QB, the spin-up band constitutes the valence band and the top of the valence band is found at point Γ, while the spin-down band constitutes the conduction band and the bottom of the conduction band is found at point Z. This indicates that the M-QB nanoribbons are indirect band gap bipolar magnetic semiconductors (BMSs) [48–52]. The bottom of the conduction band is closer to the Fermi energy level than the top of valence band, so these nanoribbons have n-type semiconductor characteristics. For M-QN, the spin-up band constitutes the valence band and the top of the valence band is found at point Z, while the spin-down band constitutes the conduction band and the bottom of the conduction band is found at point Γ. This indicates that the M-QN nanoribbons are also BMSs. However, the bottom of the conduction band is further away from the Fermi energy level (E f) than the top of the valence band, so these nanoribbons have p-type semiconductor characteristics. Figs. 2 and 3 show that the band gap varies very little with M, i.e., the width M has little effect on the band gap. Fig. 4 shows that the widths of the band structures become narrower as Q increases, which indicates that the localization of the electrons becomes stronger. The spin electron densities of the M-QB and M-QN structures (M = 15, Q = 6, 8, 10) are shown in Fig. 5 . As Fig. 5 clearly shows, the spin-polarized electrons are mainly distributed among the C atoms connected to B or N, and among the nearest neighbor and next neighbor N and B atoms. The central carbon atom is not polarized. These findings thus suggest that armchair-type BNCs can be used to design spin caloritronics devices.

Because the armchair-type BNCs are BMSs, two spin caloritronics devices were designed on the basis of 15-6B and 15-6N. The thermal spin-polarized current versus T L characteristics of the 15-6B device at three different temperature differences (ΔT = 2 K, 4 K, and 6 K) are shown in Fig. 6 (a). As the characteristics show, there is no spin current in the device when T L is less than 225 K. However, when T L rises to 225 K, the thermal spin-polarization current increases rapidly, meaning that the device has a threshold temperature (T th). The spin-up currents (I ↑) are always positive and the spin-down currents (I ↓) are always negative, thus indicating that the directions of the currents with different spin polarization orientations are opposite. Additionally, these spin-polarized currents only occur because of the temperature difference between the left and right electrodes; i.e., there is a significant SDSE. The curves of I ↑/I ↓ versus ΔT, where T L = 300 K, 350 K and 400 K, are shown in Fig. 6(d). We also observed that the I ↑ values are always positive while the I ↓ values are always negative at a positive temperature difference, thus confirming the existence of the SDSE in the device. The characteristics of the spin currents I s (I s = I up − I dn) and charge currents I c (I c = I up + I dn) versus T L and ΔT are shown in Fig. 6(b) and (c) and Fig. 6(e) and (f), respectively. It was observed that I s and I c show the same change trend and both also show diode effects, i.e., SDSD and thermoelectric diode effects. However, I s has a more obvious effect; I s ≈ 16I c in Fig. 6(b) and (c), while I s ≈ 12I c in Fig. 6(e) and (f), indicating that I s plays a key role in the device. The characteristics of I ↑/I ↓, I s and I c versus T L/ΔT of the 15-6N device are shown in Fig. 7 . Strikingly, this device has similar rules to those of the 15-6B-based device. The only differences are that the threshold temperature is 230 K, that I s ≈ −4I c in Fig. 7(b) and (c), and that I s ≈ −6I c in Fig. 7(e) and (f).

The physical mechanisms of the SDSE and the SDSD effects can be explained using Fu’s theories [53,54]. The central scattering regions of the two devices have the same structures as the two leads and thus have the same band structures. The four-spin splitting band structures around E f lead to a spin-dependent transmission spectrum, as shown in Fig. 8 . This spin-dependent transmission spectrum has two conductive channels: one is located below E f because of the spin-up sub-bands, and the other is located above E f because of the spin-down sub-bands. From Eq. (2), we find that the densities of the thermally excited electrons (e−) or holes (h+) are different when the temperatures of the two leads are different. If T L > T R, then the densities of the electrons and holes in the left lead are higher than those in the right lead, causing the electrons and holes to flow from the left lead to the right lead. Because the transport properties of the device are determined by the carriers (electrons or holes) around E f, we focused on the low-energy transmission spectrum. As mentioned earlier, there is only one channel for the spin-up electrons below E f, and one channel for the spin-down electrons above E f; therefore, the spin symmetry for the electron-hole transmissions is broken. As a result, both the net spin-down electrons and spin-up holes move from the left to the right lead, leading to the spin-up currents and spin-down currents having opposite directions and resulting in the SDSE.

The transmission channels only open when T L > T th in the presence of a spin-splitting band gap. When T L < T th (particularly when ΔT < 0), the electron and hole densities in the left lead are low and the transmission channels are almost closed. For this reason, if we fix T R at a given value, the transmission channels are then opened and the spin-dependent currents occur only when T L increases to the value of T th. Therefore, a diode effect occurs for the spin-dependent currents. For the same mechanism, the I c-ΔT curves also show an excellent thermoelectric diode effect. I c is certainly much lower than I s over the whole range of ΔT, so devices based on the armchair-type BNCs are most suitable for use in spin caloritronics devices.

In summary, using first-principles calculations in combination with the non-equilibrium Green’s function, we systematically investigated the electronic structures and thermal spin-dependent transport properties of armchair-type BNCs. First, it was shown that all the BNCs nanoribbons were indirect BMSs. Then, two spin caloritronics devices were designed based on 15-6B and 15-6N nanoribbons. The SDSE, SDSD effects and thermoelectric diode effects occurred in these devices. The physical mechanism was then explained using the Fermi-Dirac distribution and the spin transmission spectrum. These findings provide valuable theoretical guidance for the design of new spin caloritronics devices.
Tan put forward the conceptualization and wrote original draft. Zhang carried out the data curation. All authors reviewed the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China with grant No. 11864011.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G.E. Bauer, E. Saitoh, B.J. Van Wees, Spin caloritronics, Nature materials 11(5) (2012) 391.
S.R. Boona, R.C. Myers, J.P. Heremans, Spin caloritronics, Energy & Environmental Science 7(3) (2014) 885-910.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Observation of the spin Seebeck effect

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Uchida, S. Takahashi, K. Harii, J. Ieda, W. Koshibae, K. Ando, S. Maekawa, E. Saitoh, Observation of the spin Seebeck effect, Nature 455(7214) (2008) 778.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Observation of longitudinal spin-Seebeck effect in magnetic insulators

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.-i. Uchida, H. Adachi, T. Ota, H. Nakayama, S. Maekawa, E. Saitoh, Observation of longitudinal spin-Seebeck effect in magnetic insulators, Applied Physics Letters 97(17) (2010) 172505.
K.-i. Uchida, J. Xiao, H. Adachi, J.-i. Ohe, S. Takahashi, J. Ieda, T. Ota, Y. Kajiwara, H. Umezawa, H. Kawai, Spin seebeck insulator, Nature materials 9(11) (2010) 894-897.
M. Zeng, Y. Feng, G. Liang, Graphene-based spin caloritronics, Nano letters 11(3) (2011) 1369-1373.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Giant spin Seebeck effect in a non-magnetic material

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Jaworski, R. Myers, E. Johnston-Halperin, J. Heremans, Giant spin Seebeck effect in a non-magnetic material, Nature 487(7406) (2012) 210.
S.M. Wu, W. Zhang, K. Amit, P. Borisov, J.E. Pearson, J.S. Jiang, D. Lederman, A. Hoffmann, A. Bhattacharya, Antiferromagnetic spin Seebeck effect, Physical review letters 116(9) (2016) 097204.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanoporous graphene nanoribbons: robust spin-semiconducting property and perfect spin seebeck effects

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Dong, Y. Wu, X. Wang, X. Yang, Y. Liu, Nanoporous Graphene Nanoribbons: Robust Spin-Semiconducting Property and Perfect Spin Seebeck Effects, The Journal of Physical Chemistry C 123(48) (2019) 29126-29132.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Edge-defect induced spin-dependent Seebeck effect and spin figure of merit in graphene nanoribbons

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q.-B. Liu, D.-D. Wu, H.-H. Fu, Edge-defect induced spin-dependent Seebeck effect and spin figure of merit in graphene nanoribbons, Physical Chemistry Chemical Physics 19(39) (2017) 27132-27139.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spin caloritronics of blue phosphorene nanoribbons

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Liu, X. Zhang, X. Yang, X. Hong, J. Feng, M. Si, X. Wang, Spin caloritronics of blue phosphorene nanoribbons, Physical Chemistry Chemical Physics 17(16) (2015) 10462-10467.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced spin thermoelectric effects in BN-embedded zigzag graphene nanoribbons

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Liu, W. Zhou, J. Feng, X. Wang, Enhanced spin thermoelectric effects in BN-embedded zigzag graphene nanoribbons, Chemical Physics Letters 625 (2015) 14-19.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spin-resolved Fano resonances induced large spin Seebeck effects in graphene-carbon-chain junctions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.-S. Liu, X. Zhang, J.-F. Feng, X.-F. Wang, Spin-resolved Fano resonances induced large spin Seebeck effects in graphene-carbon-chain junctions, Applied Physics Letters 104(24) (2014) 242412.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spin caloritronics: Electron spins blow hot and cold

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.T. Goennenwein, G.E. Bauer, Spin caloritronics: Electron spins blow hot and cold, Nature nanotechnology 7(3) (2012) 145.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spin-dependent Seebeck effects in a graphene superlattice p–n junction with different shapes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Zhou, B. Zhou, Y. Yao, G. Zhou, M. Hu, Spin-dependent Seebeck effects in a graphene superlattice p–n junction with different shapes, Journal of Physics: Condensed Matter 29(40) (2017) 405303.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spin-dependent electronic and thermoelectric transport properties for a sawtoothlike graphene nanoribbon coupled to two ferromagnetic leads

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Zhou, B. Zhou, Y. Zeng, Spin-Dependent Electronic and Thermoelectric Transport Properties for a Sawtoothlike Graphene Nanoribbon Coupled to Two Ferromagnetic Leads, Journal of Low Temperature Physics (2019) 1-14.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spin-dependent Seebeck effects in a graphene nanoribbon coupled to two square lattice ferromagnetic leads

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Zhou, B. Zhou, Y. Zeng, G. Zhou, T. Ouyang, Spin-dependent Seebeck effects in a graphene nanoribbon coupled to two square lattice ferromagnetic leads, Journal of Applied Physics 117(10) (2015) 104305.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spin-dependent transport properties and Seebeck effects for a crossed graphene superlattice pn junction with armchair edge

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B.-H. Zhou, B.-L. Zhou, Y.-S. Zeng, M.-Y. Duan, G.-H. Zhou, Spin-dependent transport properties and Seebeck effects for a crossed graphene superlattice pn junction with armchair edge, Frontiers of Physics 13(4) (2018) 137304.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Thermodynamic analysis of interfacial transport and of the thermomagnetoelectric system

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Johnson, R. Silsbee, Thermodynamic analysis of interfacial transport and of the thermomagnetoelectric system, Physical Review B 35(10) (1987) 4959.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Johnson, R. Silsbee, Coupling of electronic charge and spin at a ferromagnetic-paramagnetic metal interface, Physical Review B 37(10) (1988) 5312.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spin-orbit coupling induced robust spin-Seebeck effect and pure thermal spin currents in achiral molecule systems

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.-H. Fu, G.-F. Du, D.-D. Wu, Q.-B. Liu, R. Wu, Spin-orbit coupling induced robust spin-Seebeck effect and pure thermal spin currents in achiral molecule systems, Physical Review B 100(8) (2019) 085407.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spin-dependent Seebeck effect, thermal colossal magnetoresistance and negative differential thermoelectric resistance in zigzag silicene nanoribbon heterojunciton

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.-H. Fu, D.-D. Wu, Z.-Q. Zhang, L. Gu, Spin-dependent Seebeck effect, thermal colossal magnetoresistance and negative differential thermoelectric resistance in zigzag silicene nanoribbon heterojunciton, Scientific reports 5 (2015) 10547.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Thermal spin filtering, thermal spin switching and negative-differential-resistance in thermal spin currents in zigzag SiC nanoribbons

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D.-D. Wu, H.-H. Fu, L. Gu, Y. Ni, F.-X. Zu, K.-L. Yao, Thermal spin filtering, thermal spin switching and negative-differential-resistance in thermal spin currents in zigzag SiC nanoribbons, Physical Chemistry Chemical Physics 16(33) (2014) 17493-17498.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Magnetic nanotubes: A new material platform to realize a robust spin-Seebeck effect and a perfect thermal spin-filtering effect

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D.-D. Wu, H.-H. Fu, Q.-B. Liu, G.-F. Du, R. Wu, Magnetic nanotubes: A new material platform to realize a robust spin-Seebeck effect and a perfect thermal spin-filtering effect, Physical Review B 98(11) (2018) 115422.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: How to realize a spin-dependent Seebeck diode effect in metallic zigzag γ-graphyne nanoribbons?

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D.-D. Wu, Q.-B. Liu, H.-H. Fu, R. Wu, How to realize a spin-dependent Seebeck diode effect in metallic zigzag γ-graphyne nanoribbons?, Nanoscale 9(46) (2017) 18334-18342.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.S. Novoselov, A.K. Geim, S.V. Morozov, D. Jiang, Y. Zhang, S.V. Dubonos, I.V. Grigorieva, A.A. Firsov, Electric field effect in atomically thin carbon films, science 306(5696) (2004) 666-669.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.S. Lee, S.H. Choi, S.J. Yun, Y.I. Kim, S. Boandoh, J.-H. Park, B.G. Shin, H. Ko, S.H. Lee, Y.-M. Kim, Wafer-scale single-crystal hexagonal boron nitride film via self-collimated grain formation, Science 362(6416) (2018) 817-821.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.A. Balandin, S. Ghosh, W. Bao, I. Calizo, D. Teweldebrhan, F. Miao, C.N. Lau, Superior thermal conductivity of single-layer graphene, Nano letters 8(3) (2008) 902-907.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electronic and optical properties of h-BN nanosheet: a first principles calculation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Beiranvand, S. Valedbagi, Electronic and optical properties of h-BN nanosheet: A first principles calculation, Diamond and Related Materials 58 (2015) 190-195.

Costa, Origin of the Exclusive Ternary Electroluminescent Behavior of BN‐Doped Nanographenes in Efficient Single‐Component White Light‐Emitting Electrochemical Cells, Advanced Functional Materials (2020) 1906830.
